name: The Conversion of Saint Paul, Apostle
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries. This feast, kept since at least the eighth century, is unusual in commemorating an event in the saint's life, his conversion, rather than the day of his death.
history: Saul of Tarsus was a zealous Pharisee and a Roman citizen who fiercely persecuted the early Church and approved the stoning of Saint Stephen. While traveling to Damascus to arrest Christians, he was struck to the ground by a light from heaven and heard the risen Lord say, "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?" The Acts of the Apostles recounts the event three times, and from that day the persecutor became the great Apostle to the Gentiles.
contributions: Paul carried the Gospel across the Mediterranean on three missionary journeys and gave the Church a large portion of the New Testament through his letters, the Church's earliest theology. His conversion stands for all time as the sign that no one is beyond the reach of God's grace.
patronage: Missionaries, evangelists, and writers; invoked by all who pray for conversion.
feast_engagement: Read one of the three accounts of the conversion in Acts (chapters 9, 22, or 26). Pray for someone far from the faith, trusting that the God who turned Saul into Paul can reach anyone.
summary: On January 25 the Church recalls the conversion of Saint Paul, struck down on the road to Damascus and raised up as the Apostle to the Gentiles. His sudden change from persecutor to preacher is the enduring sign that grace can transform any heart.

name: The Chair of Saint Peter, Apostle
canonization: Feast origin. This celebration was already kept in Rome before the fourth century under the name Natale Petri de Cathedra, honoring the office of Peter rather than a day of death.
history: The "chair," in Latin cathedra, is not first of all a piece of furniture but the teaching office and authority given by Christ to Peter when he said, "You are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my Church." That office passed to each of Peter's successors as Bishop of Rome. A wooden chair long associated with the Apostle is enshrined in Bernini's great bronze monument in Saint Peter's Basilica, a visible sign of an invisible reality.
contributions: The feast celebrates the unity of the Church gathered around the successor of Peter, the visible source and foundation of communion among the bishops and the faithful. It reminds Catholics that Christ governs and teaches his Church through a living, ongoing office.
patronage: No patronage; this feast honors the office of Peter and the unity of the Church.
feast_engagement: Pray for the Pope and for the unity of the Church. Renew your trust in the Church's teaching authority, which guards the deposit of faith handed down from the Apostles.
summary: On February 22 the Church honors the Chair of Saint Peter, the teaching office Christ entrusted to Peter and to his successors as Bishop of Rome. It is a feast of the Church's unity, a call to pray for the Pope and to hold fast to the apostolic faith.

name: Saint Mark, Evangelist
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries (c. 12 to c. 68).
history: Though little is known of his life with certainty, Mark is the author of the second Gospel, a swift and vivid account widely believed to rest on the preaching of Saint Peter, who calls him "my son" in his first letter. He was a companion of Paul and Barnabas and later a close helper of Paul in Rome. Tradition holds that he was sent to Alexandria in Egypt, founded the Church there, and was martyred under Nero.
contributions: Mark gave the Church the shortest and swiftest of the four Gospels and is honored as the founder of the Church of Alexandria, the first to bring the Gospel to the continent of Africa. His symbol is the winged lion, drawn from the four living creatures of Scripture.
patronage: Notaries, Egypt, and the city of Venice, which holds his relics.
feast_engagement: Read a chapter of Mark's Gospel, noticing its urgency and its repeated word "immediately." Give thanks for the evangelists who handed on the life of Christ in writing.
summary: On April 25 the Church honors Saint Mark, who wrote the second Gospel from the preaching of Peter and carried the faith to Alexandria. His winged lion is a familiar sign of the evangelist who told the story of Jesus with vivid speed.

name: Saints Philip and James, Apostles
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries. Both are named in the Roman Canon (Eucharistic Prayer I).
history: Philip, like Peter and Andrew, was from Bethsaida; at the Last Supper he asked, "Master, show us the Father," and Jesus answered that to see him is to see the Father. Tradition says Philip later preached in Greece and Phrygia and was martyred at Hierapolis. James, called the Less, was a relative of the Lord and became the first bishop of Jerusalem, presiding at the Council of Jerusalem in Acts 15; he is traditionally held to have written the Letter of James and to have been martyred by being thrown from the Temple and clubbed.
contributions: Philip teaches the Church to seek the Father in the face of Christ, while James guided the mother Church of Jerusalem and left a letter on living faith through good works. The two are honored together because their relics rest in the same Roman church.
patronage: Philip is invoked by hatters and pastry cooks; James by pharmacists; both as faithful witnesses of the Lord.
feast_engagement: Read Jesus' words to Philip in John 14, and a passage from the Letter of James on faith expressed in action. Ask these apostles to deepen both your vision of Christ and your works of charity.
summary: On May 3 the Church honors the apostles Philip and James. Philip, who longed to see the Father, and James, first bishop of Jerusalem and writer on living faith, are remembered together as pillars of the early Church.

name: Saint Matthias, Apostle
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries.
history: After the Ascension, the eleven apostles gathered to choose a replacement for Judas, requiring one who had been with them from the baptism of John until the day the Lord was taken up. Two men were proposed, and after prayer the lot fell to Matthias, who was numbered with the Twelve (Acts 1:15 to 26). His feast was moved in 1969 to May 14 so it would fall in Eastertide, near the Ascension at which Acts places his choosing.
contributions: Matthias completed the college of the Twelve, a sign that the Church carries on the apostolic mission entrusted by Christ. Chosen by lot after prayer, he stands for every disciple called by God's providence rather than human ambition.
patronage: Carpenters, tailors, and those who struggle with addiction; invoked for perseverance.
feast_engagement: Read the account of his choosing in the first chapter of Acts. Pray for openness to God's call in your own life, trusting that he assigns each person a place in his plan.
summary: On May 14 the Church honors Saint Matthias, chosen by lot to take the place of Judas among the Twelve. Having followed the Lord from his baptism to his Ascension, Matthias shows that the apostolic mission is handed on by God's own choosing.

name: The Visitation of the Blessed Virgin Mary
canonization: Feast origin. Adopted by the Franciscans in 1263 and extended to the whole Church by Pope Urban VI in 1389, the feast was fixed at May 31 in the reform after the Second Vatican Council, at the close of the month dedicated to Mary.
history: After the Annunciation, Mary set out in haste to the hill country to visit her cousin Elizabeth, who was with child in her old age (Luke 1:39 to 56). At Mary's greeting the infant John leapt for joy in Elizabeth's womb, and Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit, cried out, "Most blessed are you among women." Mary answered with the Magnificat, her great song of praise, "My soul proclaims the greatness of the Lord."
contributions: The Visitation gives the Church the Magnificat, prayed every evening in the Church's official prayer, and shows Mary as the first to carry Christ to others. Set between the Annunciation and the birth of John the Baptist, it joins the two great events of the Lord's coming.
patronage: No patronage; this is a feast of the Blessed Virgin Mary.
feast_engagement: Pray the Magnificat slowly, or the second Joyful Mystery of the Rosary. In imitation of Mary's charity, visit someone who is sick, elderly, or expecting a child.
summary: On May 31 the Church celebrates the Visitation, when Mary, carrying Christ in her womb, hurried to serve Elizabeth. John leapt for joy, Elizabeth proclaimed her blessed, and Mary sang the Magnificat, the Church's evening hymn of praise.

name: Saint Thomas, Apostle
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries. His feast was formerly December 21 and was moved to July 3 in 1969.
history: Thomas, called Didymus, the twin, is best known for refusing to believe in the Resurrection until he could see and touch the wounds of the Lord. A week later the risen Jesus appeared and invited him to touch, and Thomas made the great confession, "My Lord and my God." Tradition holds that he carried the Gospel as far as India, where Christian communities still trace their origin to him, and where he was martyred near present-day Chennai.
contributions: Thomas's confession is one of the clearest professions of Christ's divinity in the Gospels, and the faithful echo his words at Mass. Saint Gregory the Great observed that his doubt, once overcome, has strengthened the faith of believers more than the ready belief of the others.
patronage: India, architects and builders, and those who struggle with doubt.
feast_engagement: Read the account of his confession in John 20, and make his words, "My Lord and my God," your own prayer at the elevation of the Host. Bring your own doubts honestly to Christ, who met Thomas with mercy.
summary: On July 3 the Church honors Saint Thomas, who doubted the Resurrection until he touched the wounds of the risen Lord and cried, "My Lord and my God." He carried the Gospel to India and died a martyr, the patron of all who seek faith through their questions.

name: Saint Mary Magdalene
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since early times. Pope Francis raised her celebration from a memorial to a Feast in 2016, giving her the same rank as the apostles, with a proper Preface naming her "Apostle to the Apostles."
history: Mary of Magdala was healed by Jesus of seven demons and became a faithful follower who supported his ministry from her own means. She stood by the Cross and came to the tomb on Easter morning, where the risen Lord appeared to her in the garden and called her by name. He sent her to announce his Resurrection to the disciples, and she went and told them, "I have seen the Lord."
contributions: Because she was the first witness of the Resurrection and the first sent to proclaim it, Saint Thomas Aquinas and others called her apostola apostolorum, the Apostle to the Apostles. Her life is a sign of Christ's mercy and of the dignity of every disciple called to bear witness to the risen Lord.
patronage: Penitents, converts, and those seeking God's mercy.
feast_engagement: Read the garden encounter in John 20. Ponder God's unlimited mercy, which raised this woman to be the first herald of Easter, and ask for the grace to proclaim the living Christ.
summary: On July 22 the Church honors Saint Mary Magdalene, healed by Christ, faithful at the Cross, and the first to see and proclaim the risen Lord. Pope Francis raised her day to a Feast in 2016, honoring the Apostle to the Apostles.

name: Saint James, Apostle
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries (first century to c. 44).
history: James, the son of Zebedee and brother of John, left his nets to follow the Lord and became, with Peter and his brother, one of the three closest to Jesus, present at the Transfiguration and in the Garden of Gethsemane. He is called James the Greater to distinguish him from the other apostle of the same name. He was the first of the Twelve to be martyred, beheaded by Herod Agrippa around the year 44, as recorded in Acts 12.
contributions: James gave the supreme witness of martyrdom early in the Church's life, drinking the cup the Lord had foretold he would share. His shrine at Santiago de Compostela in Spain has drawn pilgrims for over a thousand years along the Camino, the Way of Saint James.
feast_engagement: Read of his call and his martyrdom in the Gospels and in Acts 12. If you have ever longed to make a pilgrimage, offer a prayer today along the spirit of the Camino, walking toward Christ.
summary: On July 25 the Church honors Saint James the Greater, one of the three closest to Jesus and the first apostle to die for the faith. His shrine at Compostela has drawn pilgrims for centuries along the famous Camino.

name: Saint Lawrence, Deacon and Martyr
canonization: Pre-Congregation, highly venerated as a martyr since at least the fourth century (c. 225 to 258).
history: Lawrence was one of the seven deacons of Rome, entrusted with the goods of the Church and the care of the poor. During the persecution of Valerian in 258, Pope Sixtus II and several deacons were martyred, and Lawrence was told to hand over the treasures of the Church. He asked for three days, gathered the poor, the sick, and the suffering, and presented them to the prefect, saying, "These are the treasures of the Church." For this he was put to death, by ancient tradition roasted on a gridiron.
contributions: Lawrence's witness joined service of the poor to the offering of his own life, and his courage is credited with strengthening the faith of Rome. He is one of the most honored of the early Roman martyrs, remembered for his charity and his joy in the face of death.
patronage: Deacons, the poor, cooks, and firefighters.
feast_engagement: Reflect on his words that the poor are the Church's true treasure, and give an alms or an act of service today. Pray for deacons, who serve the Church at the altar and among the needy.
summary: On August 10 the Church honors Saint Lawrence, the deacon of Rome who gave the Church's wealth to the poor and called them her true treasure. Martyred under Valerian, he is the beloved patron of deacons and the poor.

name: Saints Michael, Gabriel, and Raphael, Archangels
canonization: Not applicable, since angels are not canonized. The 1969 calendar reform combined three older celebrations of the archangels into this single Feast.
history: The three archangels named in Scripture are honored together on this day. Michael, whose name means "Who is like God," is the protector who casts down Satan and defends the people of God, as in the Book of Revelation. Gabriel, "God is my strength," is the messenger who announced the births of John the Baptist and of the Lord himself to Mary. Raphael, "God has healed," is the companion and healer who guides Tobias and restores sight to Tobit in the Book of Tobit.
contributions: The feast teaches that God surrounds his people with angelic protection, guidance, and healing, and that the unseen world serves his saving plan. The archangels are honored as ministers who stand before God and carry out his will.
patronage: Michael of soldiers, police, and the dying; Gabriel of messengers and communication; Raphael of travelers, the sick, and happy meetings.
feast_engagement: Pray the prayer to Saint Michael for protection, and ask Raphael's guidance for any journey or healing you need. Give thanks for the angels who serve God and watch over us.
summary: On September 29 the Church honors the three archangels named in Scripture: Michael the protector, Gabriel the messenger, and Raphael the healer. The feast celebrates God's care for his people through his unseen ministers.

name: Saint Luke, Evangelist
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries (first century, died about age 84).
history: Luke was a Greek physician from Antioch, most likely a Gentile convert and the only one of the four evangelists not of Jewish origin. He became a faithful companion of Saint Paul, joining him on his journeys, as the "we" passages of Acts reveal, and staying with him to the end; from his Roman prison Paul wrote, "Only Luke is with me." Luke wrote the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles, together the largest single contribution to the New Testament.
contributions: To Luke we owe much of what we know of the infancy of Christ, the Blessed Mother, and the tender parables of mercy such as the Prodigal Son, as well as the entire early history of the Church in Acts. His symbol is the winged ox, and tradition honors him as the first iconographer of Our Lady, making him the patron of artists.
feast_engagement: Read one of the parables found only in Luke, such as the Good Samaritan or the Prodigal Son. Pray for doctors, nurses, and artists, whom this evangelist patrons.
summary: On October 18 the Church honors Saint Luke, the physician who became Paul's faithful companion and wrote the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles. We owe to him the infancy of Christ and the great parables of mercy.

name: The Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed (All Souls)
canonization: Feast origin. Prayer for the dead is ancient, attested in Scripture (2 Maccabees 12) and in the catacombs; Saint Odilo, abbot of Cluny, set November 2 for this commemoration in his monasteries around the year 998, and the custom spread to the whole Western Church.
history: The day after All Saints, the Church prays for all the faithful departed who have died in God's grace but are not yet fully purified, the souls in purgatory. The doctrine rests on the communion of saints, the bond uniting the Church in heaven, the Church on earth, and the Church being purified. The faithful help these souls by prayer, by almsgiving, and above all by the offering of the Mass.
contributions: All Souls expresses the Church's hope that death does not sever the bonds of love, and that the living can aid the dead toward heaven. A plenary indulgence for the holy souls may be gained by visiting a cemetery and praying for the departed during the first eight days of November.
patronage: No patronage; this is the Church's day of prayer for all the dead.
feast_engagement: Offer Mass, the Rosary, or the prayer "Eternal rest grant unto them, O Lord" for your departed loved ones. Visit a cemetery and pray for the dead, gaining the indulgence offered for the holy souls.
summary: On November 2 the Church prays for all the faithful departed, the souls being purified before they see God. Rooted in the communion of saints, the day calls the living to help the dead through prayer, almsgiving, and the Mass.

name: Saint John, Apostle and Evangelist
canonization: Pre-Congregation, venerated since the earliest centuries (early first century to c. 101).
history: John, the son of Zebedee and brother of James, was called from his nets and became "the beloved disciple," one of the three closest to Jesus. He alone of the apostles stood at the foot of the Cross, where the dying Lord entrusted his Mother to John's care. He wrote the fourth Gospel, three letters, and the Book of Revelation, the last written during exile on the island of Patmos under Domitian, and he is held to have died in old age at Ephesus, the only apostle not to be martyred.
contributions: John gave the Church the most exalted Gospel, opening with the eternal Word who became flesh, and is honored as the evangelist of the divinity of Christ and of fraternal love. An ancient story tells that in his last years he repeated to his flock simply, "My little children, love one another."
patronage: Theologians, writers, and editors; his symbol is the eagle.
feast_engagement: Read the prologue of John's Gospel, "In the beginning was the Word." Take to heart his final counsel and perform some concrete act of love for another today.
summary: On December 27 the Church honors Saint John, the beloved disciple who stood at the Cross, received Mary into his care, and wrote the fourth Gospel, three letters, and Revelation. The evangelist of the Word made flesh, he taught above all that we must love one another.
